title: Pulp Fiction
logline: The lives of two mob hitmen, a boxer, a gangster's wife, and a pair of diner bandits intertwine in four tales of violence and redemption in Los Angeles.
protagonist: Multiple protagonists (Jules Winnfield, Vincent Vega, Butch Coolidge) in an ensemble structure
antagonistOrConflict: Fate, moral choice, and the consequences of violence in the criminal underworld
tone: Darkly comic, stylized, conversational, violent yet philosophical
themes: The randomness of fate versus divine intervention

actBreaks.description: Vincent delivers Mia home safely after the overdose crisis, completing the first major narrative thread and transitioning from the Vincent/Mia story to Butch's story.

validation.ruling: Scene #41 (p54) ends Act 1 as Vincent and Mia agree to keep the overdose incident secret from Marsellus, fundamentally shifting the story from the date assignment to its dangerous aftermath and establishing a new conspiracy of silence that cannot be undone.

actBreaks.description: Butch and Fabienne prepare to escape on the chopper, seemingly free, before the final act reveals the unresolved cleanup job that circles back to the opening.

validation.ruling: Scene #72 (p91) ends Act 2 as Butch retrieves Fabienne and prepares to escape on the stolen chopper, completing his revenge/survival arc and transitioning the narrative to the final Jules-centered resolution segment where themes of redemption will be explored.

naturalStructure.structuralPersonality:
This script employs a radical non-linear, chapter-based structure that fragments chronology to create thematic resonance rather than traditional causality. The film opens and closes in the same diner (scenes 1 and 92-94), creating a circular frame, but the interior chapters are deliberately scrambled: we see Jules and Vincent's morning hit job (scenes 2-8), then jump to Vincent's date with Mia that same evening (scenes 9-41), then leap to Butch's story days later (scenes 42-72), before finally returning to the immediate aftermath of the opening hit (scenes 73-94). This structure allows the script to explore variations on themes—divine intervention, loyalty, redemption—across different characters while maintaining tonal unity through style and dialogue rather than plot progression.

The chapter titles function as act breaks within the non-linear framework: 'Vincent Vega and Marsellus Wallace's Wife' (beginning scene 9), 'The Gold Watch' (beginning scene 42), and 'The Bonnie Situation' (beginning scene 73). Each chapter operates as a self-contained short film with its own arc, yet they're interconnected through character crossovers and the briefcase MacGuffin. The genius lies in how the scrambled chronology creates dramatic irony—we see Vincent alive in the diner finale even though we've already witnessed his death in Butch's apartment.
naturalStructure.pacingNotes: The pacing is deceptively leisurely, prioritizing extended dialogue scenes over action. The opening apartment hit (scene 8) doesn't arrive until page 15, after 13 pages of conversation. The Mia/Vincent section luxuriates in the Jackrabbit Slim's dinner (scenes 29-31, pages 31-42) before the overdose crisis accelerates the tempo (scenes 36-39). Butch's chapter moves fastest, with rapid location changes (scenes 53-63) building to the pawnshop confrontation (scenes 64-69). The final diner sequence (scenes 92-94) returns to the talky rhythm of the opening, creating symmetry. The script uses scene length strategically: intimate two-handers run long (3-11 pages), while action beats are compressed into quick cuts.

incitingIncident.description: Jules and Vincent enter an apartment where three young men are eating hamburgers; Jules interrogates Brett about a briefcase while Vincent retrieves it from another room, then Jules recites a biblical passage before he and Vincent shoot Brett and another man.

validation.ruling: Scene #8 (p15) shatters the ordinary world as Jules and Vincent confront Brett and his associates about their business with Marsellus Wallace, establishing the main conflict of retrieving the briefcase and introducing the irreversible stakes of criminal enforcement that drives the narrative forward.

midpoint.description: English Dave enters Floyd Ray Willis's locker room where the boxer lies dead on a table with his trainer crying over the body, while Marsellus's large figure stands nearby and Vincent greets Mia.

validation.ruling: Scene #52 is intimate character development but lacks the structural shift required for a midpoint. Scene #48 (p61, 48%) is the true midpoint where Butch learns the boxer he was supposed to throw the fight against is dead—a point of no return that transforms him from reactive fugitive to proactive survivor, raising stakes from financial gain to life-or-death consequences and forcing irreversible choices.

climax.description: Pumpkin collects wallets in a trash bag during a robbery; when he reaches Jules and demands the briefcase, Jules refuses, Pumpkin aims his gun at Jules' face, but Jules reveals his own gun and takes control, disarms both robbers, and delivers a speech about his spiritual transformation before letting them leave with the cash but keeping the briefcase.

validation.ruling: Scene #94 (p119) is the climax where Jules directly confronts Pumpkin at gunpoint over the briefcase, demonstrating his completed character arc by choosing mercy and walking away from violence—the highest tension confrontation that resolves his spiritual transformation and pays off the miracle theme established earlier.

scenes.heading: INT. COFFEE SHOP – MORNING
scenes.summary: A young man and young woman sit in a coffee shop debating whether to continue committing robberies, with the man insisting he's done with risky jobs while the woman challenges his resolve.

scenes.heading: INT. '74 CHEVY (MOVING) – MORNING
scenes.summary: Vincent and Jules drive through Hollywood discussing Amsterdam's hash bars and the legality of marijuana in Europe.

scenes.heading: INT. CHEVY (TRUNK) – MORNING
scenes.summary: Jules and Vincent open the trunk of their car, retrieve two .45 automatics, load and cock them, and discuss wishing they had shotguns for the job ahead.

scenes.heading: EXT. APARTMENT BUILDING COURTYARD – MORNING
scenes.summary: Vincent and Jules walk through an apartment courtyard discussing Mia Wallace, Marsellus's wife who used to be an actress and starred in a pilot.

scenes.heading: INT. RECEPTION AREA (APARTMENT BUILDING) – MORNING
scenes.summary: Vincent and Jules wait for the elevator while Jules tells Vincent that Marsellus had someone thrown off a balcony for giving Mia a foot massage.

scenes.heading: INT. ELEVATOR – MORNING
scenes.summary: In the elevator, Jules explains that Marsellus sent men to throw Tony Rocky Horror off a fourth-story balcony through a greenhouse for giving Mia a foot massage.

scenes.heading: INT. APARTMENT BUILDING HALLWAY – MORNING
scenes.summary: Walking down the hallway, Vincent and Jules debate whether a foot massage is intimate enough to justify Marsellus's violent reaction, with Jules insisting it's nothing while Vincent argues it's in the same ballpark as more intimate acts.

scenes.heading: INT. APARTMENT (ROOM 49) – MORNING
scenes.summary: Jules and Vincent enter an apartment where three young men are eating hamburgers; Jules interrogates Brett about a briefcase while Vincent retrieves it from another room, then Jules recites a biblical passage before he and Vincent shoot Brett and another man.
scenes.turningPointNote: First major act of violence — Jules and Vincent execute their hit, establishing the film's violent criminal world and the characters' roles as hitmen

scenes.heading: INT. CAR (MOVING) – DAY
scenes.summary: Vincent drives a cherry-red Chevy Malibu convertible with rockabilly music playing on the radio.

scenes.heading: EXT. SALLY LeROY'S – DAY
scenes.summary: Vincent arrives at Sally LeRoy's topless bar with a black briefcase and is greeted by English Dave at the door.

scenes.heading: INT. SALLY LeROY'S – DAY
scenes.summary: English Dave tells Vincent to wait while Marsellus finishes business with Butch, offers Vincent coffee, and warns him cryptically about taking Mia out, refusing to elaborate on what's funny about it.

scenes.heading: INT. LANCE'S HOUSE (KITCHEN) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Jody, a heavily pierced woman, discusses body piercing with Trudi at a kitchen table while Vincent sits excluded from their conversation.

scenes.heading: INT. LANCE'S BEDROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Lance shows Vincent three types of heroin on his bed, describing their origins and prices, then demonstrates the quality by cooking and injecting the most expensive variety.

scenes.heading: EXT. MARSELLUS WALLACE'S HOUSE – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent walks to Marsellus Wallace's house and finds a note from Mia on the door telling him to come inside and make himself a drink.

scenes.heading: INT. MARCELLUS' HOUSE / LIVING ROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent enters Marsellus's house and calls out hello.

scenes.heading: INT. MARCELLUS' HOUSE / DRESSING ROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Mia speaks Vincent's name into a microphone in the dressing room while Dusty Springfield plays.

scenes.heading: INT. MARCELLUS' HOUSE / LIVING ROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent turns around when he hears Mia's voice over the intercom.

scenes.heading: INT. MARCELLUS' HOUSE / DRESSING ROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Mia tells Vincent over the intercom where to find the intercom on the wall.

scenes.heading: INT. MARCELLUS' HOUSE / LIVING ROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Mia guides Vincent over the intercom until he finds it on the wall, then tells him to make himself a drink.

scenes.heading: INT. MARCELLUS' HOUSE / DRESSING ROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Mia tells Vincent over the microphone to make himself a drink at the bar by the fireplace.

scenes.heading: INT. MARCELLUS' HOUSE / LIVING ROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent acknowledges Mia's instruction over the intercom and licks his lips.

scenes.heading: INT. MARCELLUS' HOUSE / DRESSING ROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Mia watches Vincent on a video screen and turns a knob controlling the camera movement while the Dusty Springfield song continues.

scenes.heading: INT. MARCELLUS' HOUSE / LIVING ROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent picks up a bottle of scotch, sniffs it, and pours himself a glass.

scenes.heading: INT. MARCELLUS' HOUSE / DRESSING ROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: A razor blade cuts cocaine on a mirror in Mia's dressing room.

scenes.heading: INT. MARCELLUS' HOUSE / LIVING ROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent drinks his glass of scotch.

scenes.heading: INT. MARCELLUS' HOUSE / DRESSING ROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Mia sniffs the cocaine.

scenes.heading: INT. MARCELLUS' HOUSE / LIVING ROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent finishes his drink and looks at a portrait of Mia, then Mia enters the room, stops the record, and tells him they should go.

scenes.heading: EXT. JACKRABBIT SLIM'S – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent's Malibu pulls up to Jackrabbit Slim's, an elaborate 1950s-themed diner.

scenes.heading: INT. JACKRABBIT SLIM'S – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent and Mia sit in a booth at the elaborately themed 1950s restaurant where their waiter Buddy Holly takes their order; they discuss Marilyn Monroe impersonators, and Mia asks Vincent about a rumor involving her and Tony Rocky Horror.

scenes.heading: INT. JACKRABBIT SLIM'S (LADIES ROOM) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Mia snorts a large line of cocaine off the bathroom sink and her head jerks back from the rush.

scenes.heading: INT. JACKRABBIT SLIM'S (DINING AREA) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent eats his steak while Mia returns from the bathroom; they banter about the waiters, debate Marilyn Monroe versus Mamie Van Doren, and Vincent asks Mia about the rumor that she had someone thrown out a window, which she denies and explains was about a failed TV pilot.

scenes.heading: INT. MARSELLUS WALLACE'S HOME – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Mia and Vincent dance into Marsellus's house singing, then stand face to face in an uncomfortable silence before Mia suggests music and drinks while Vincent goes to the bathroom.

scenes.heading: INT. BATHROOM (MARSELLUS WALLACE'S HOUSE) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent talks to himself in the bathroom mirror about leaving quickly, while in the living room Mia snorts what she thinks is cocaine but is actually Vincent's heroin, causing a terrible reaction before she collapses unconscious.
scenes.turningPointNote: Mia overdoses on heroin — a life-threatening crisis that forces Vincent into desperate action and creates potential catastrophic consequences with Marsellus

scenes.heading: EXT.VINCENT'S HOT ROD (MOVING) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent drives at a hundred miles per hour, speeding through turns and over hills.

scenes.heading: INT.VINCENT'S HOT ROD (MOVING) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent drives maniacally with one hand on the wheel while Mia sits slack-jawed and unconscious beside him, and he pulls out a cellular phone to make a call.

scenes.heading: INT. LANCE'S HOUSE – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Lance, watching TV in his bathrobe, answers the phone and argues with Vincent about bringing someone to his house, while Jody calls from the bedroom asking about the ringing phone.

scenes.heading: EXT. LANCE'S HOUSE – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent carries Mia out of his car at Lance's house while Lance yells at him for crashing into his house and talking about drugs on a cellular phone, until Vincent reveals the woman is Marsellus Wallace's wife and threatens to implicate Lance if she dies.

scenes.heading: INT. LANCE'S HOUSE – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Jody enters the living room to find Vincent and Lance standing over the unconscious Mia; Lance sends Jody to get an adrenaline shot while Vincent and Jody argue about what to do, and Lance insists they need to give Mia the shot directly to her heart.

scenes.heading: INT. SPARE ROOM
scenes.summary: Lance frantically searches a junk-filled room for his medical book while Vincent and Jody call to him from the living room; Jody enters and argues with Lance about the messy room before finding the book and reading the instructions for administering the adrenaline shot, then they rush back to the living room where Vincent plunges the needle into Mia's chest and she violently revives.
scenes.turningPointNote: Mia is saved from death by the adrenaline shot — the climax of the overdose crisis, resolving the immediate mortal threat

scenes.heading: INT. VINCENT'S MALIBU (MOVING) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Vincent drives Mia home in silence, both still shaken from the overdose incident.

scenes.heading: EXT. FRONT OF MARSELLUS WALLACE'S HOUSE – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Mia gets out of the car and walks toward her house; Vincent calls to her and they agree to keep the overdose incident secret from Marsellus, shake hands on it, then Mia tells Vincent her Fox Force Five joke before he leaves.

scenes.heading: INT. LOCKER ROOM – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Butch Coolidge wakes from a nap in the locker room before his fight, shaken by a memory, and his trainer Klondike tells him it's time.

scenes.heading: EXT. ALLEY (RAINING) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: A taxi sits parked in a rainy alley while sportscasters on the radio discuss how quickly Coolidge left the ring and speculate that he knew Willis was dead.

scenes.heading: INT. TAXI (PARKED/RAINING) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Esmarelda Villalobos, a female cabbie, sits in her parked taxi drinking coffee while listening to sportscasters discuss a ring death tragedy and its impact on boxing.

scenes.heading: EXT. BOXING AUDITORIUM (RAINING) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Butch throws a gym bag out a window into a dumpster, leaps down after it, climbs out, removes his robe, and runs to the waiting taxi.

scenes.heading: INT. TAXI (PARKED / RAINING) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Butch, soaking wet and wearing only boxing trunks and shoes, jumps into the backseat of Esmarelda's taxi and tells her to drive away; she starts the engine and stomps on the gas.

scenes.heading: EXT. BOXING AUDITORIUM (RAINING) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: The taxi speeds out of the alley and fishtails on the wet pavement in front of the auditorium.

scenes.heading: INT. WILLIS LOCKER ROOM (AUDITORIUM) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: English Dave enters Floyd Ray Willis's locker room where the boxer lies dead on a table with his trainer crying over the body, while Marsellus's large figure stands nearby and Vincent greets Mia.
scenes.turningPointNote: Floyd Willis is revealed dead after the fight — confirming Butch killed his opponent and establishing the consequences of the fixed fight gone wrong

scenes.heading: INT. CAB (MOVING / RAINING) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Butch removes his boxing gloves and tosses them out the taxi window while Esmarelda asks if he was in the fight; he confirms he's the fighter and learns from her that the other boxer is dead, then discusses whether it feels bad to kill a man.

scenes.heading: EXT. PHONE BOOTH (RAINING) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Butch talks on a payphone about how the fixed fight odds went out of control and discusses collecting money from eight bookies by tomorrow evening, mentioning he'll be in Knoxville the next night.

scenes.heading: EXT. MOTEL (STOPPED / RAINING) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Butch pays Esmaralda the taxi fare plus a hundred-dollar tip in exchange for her silence about who her passenger was tonight.

scenes.heading: INT. MOTEL (ROOM SIX) – NIGHT
scenes.summary: Butch returns to the motel room where Fabienne waits, and they have a playful conversation in baby-talk about pot bellies and body image before cuddling in bed.

scenes.heading: INT. HONDA (MOVING) – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch drives his Honda while furiously beating the steering wheel, enraged that Fabienne forgot his father's watch despite his specific reminder.

scenes.heading: EXT. CITY STREET – DAY
scenes.summary: The Honda races toward its destination as a parking meter's flag rises to one hour.

scenes.heading: EXT. RESIDENTIAL STREET CORNER – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch parks his car a couple blocks from his apartment to cautiously check things out before approaching.

scenes.heading: EXT. ALLEY – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch walks down an alley until he reaches another street and discreetly glances out.

scenes.heading: EXT. STREET – BUTCH'S APARTMENT – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch surveys the street in front of his apartment building, observes that everything appears normal with no suspicious cars or people, then crosses the street and enters the courtyard.

scenes.heading: EXT. BUTCH'S APARTMENT COURTYARD – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch checks the courtyard and finds everything normal, climbs the stairs to apartment 12, listens at the door, then quietly unlocks and opens it.

scenes.heading: INT. BUTCH'S APARTMENT – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch enters his untouched apartment, drinks milk from the refrigerator, retrieves his father's watch from the bedside kangaroo statue, then puts Pop Tarts in the toaster.

scenes.heading: EXT. APARTMENT COURTYARD – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch walks rapidly across the courtyard, exits the building, crosses the street, goes through the alley, and reaches his car in one continuous shot.

scenes.heading: EXT. HONDA – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch cranks the car into gear and drives away with a wide smile of survival breaking across his face.

scenes.heading: EXT. APARTMENT BUILDING STREET – DAY
scenes.summary: The Honda turns down the alley and slowly cruises past Butch's apartment building.

scenes.heading: INT. HONDA – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch laughs and sings along to music while stopped at a light, then sees Marsellus Wallace exit Teriyaki Donut carrying coffee and donuts and cross directly in front of his car; Marsellus glances left, recognizes Butch, and both men freeze in mutual recognition.
scenes.turningPointNote: The hunter and hunted collide by chance — Marsellus discovers Butch's location, igniting immediate pursuit

scenes.heading: INT. MASON-DIXIE PAWNSHOP – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch races into Maynard's pawnshop followed by Marsellus; Butch punches Marsellus to the ground, takes his gun, breaks his finger, then Maynard knocks Butch unconscious with a baseball bat and aims a shotgun at the fallen Marsellus.
scenes.turningPointNote: Both protagonist and antagonist are captured by a third threat, fundamentally shifting the power dynamic

scenes.heading: INT. PAWNSHOP BACK ROOM – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch and Marsellus wake up tied to chairs with ball gags in their mouths in a dungeon-like basement; Maynard and Zed discuss who to assault first, choose Marsellus, gag Butch with duct tape, and take Marsellus into another room while leaving Butch tied up.

scenes.heading: INT. PAWNSHOP – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch sneaks to the pawnshop door, grabs keys from the counter, then stops to listen to the assault happening below and decides he cannot leave Marsellus in that situation; he searches for a weapon, rejecting a hammer and chainsaw before selecting a katana sword.
scenes.turningPointNote: Butch makes a moral choice to rescue his enemy rather than escape, transforming their relationship

scenes.heading: INT. PAWNSHOP BACK ROOM – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch quietly sneaks down the stairs to the dungeon while sounds continue from behind the closed door.

scenes.heading: INT. RUSSELL'S OLD ROOM – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch pushes open the door, sees Zed assaulting Marsellus while Maynard watches, gets Maynard's attention, slashes Maynard across the front with the sword, skewers him from behind, then points the blade at Zed's throat.

scenes.heading: EXT. MASON-DIXIE PAWNSHOP – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch exits the pawnshop still shaking, sees Zed's chrome chopper parked outside, climbs aboard, starts it with the keys, and speeds off.

scenes.heading: INT. BUTCH AND FABIENNE'S HOTEL ROOM – DAY
scenes.summary: Fabienne stands in front of a mirror in a Frankie Says Relax tee-shirt, singing along to music from a boom box.

scenes.heading: EXT. CITY STREET – CHOPPER (MOVING) – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch drives the chopper down the street and checks his father's watch, which reads 10:30.

scenes.heading: EXT. MOTEL ROOM – DAY
scenes.summary: Butch rides up on the chopper and tells Fabienne to grab her things immediately; she exits with her boom box and purse, questions him about the motorcycle and her crashed Honda, then reluctantly climbs on behind him.

scenes.heading: INT. BATHROOM – DAY
scenes.summary: The Fourth Man paces in the bathroom clutching his silver Magnum, listening intently to Jules reciting scripture on the other side of the door.

scenes.heading: INT. APARTMENT – DAY
scenes.summary: The Fourth Man bursts from the bathroom firing six shots from his Magnum at Jules and Vincent, but miraculously misses them entirely; confused, he is then shot and killed by Jules and Vincent.
scenes.turningPointNote: The miraculous survival from point-blank gunfire triggers Jules' spiritual awakening and decision to leave the criminal life

scenes.heading: EXT. HOLLYWOOD APARTMENT BUILDING – MORNING
scenes.summary: The Chevy Nova propels itself into traffic.

scenes.heading: INT. NOVA (MOVING) – MORNING
scenes.summary: Jules drives with Vincent in the passenger seat and Marvin in back; Jules insists the missed shots were divine intervention and announces he's retiring, while Vincent argues it was just a freak occurrence and accuses Jules of freaking out.

scenes.heading: INT. JIMMIE'S BATHROOM – DAY
scenes.summary: Jules washes blood from his hands while warning Vincent they must be delicate with Jimmie, who is one remark away from kicking them out; Vincent takes his place at the sink.

scenes.heading: INT. JIMMIE'S KITCHEN – MORNING
scenes.summary: Jules, Vincent, and Jimmie stand in the kitchen drinking coffee; Jimmie cuts off Jules' compliments and angrily states his concern is not the coffee but the dead person in his garage, then demands to know what will happen when his wife Bonnie comes home.

scenes.heading: INT. MARSELLUS WALLACE'S DINING ROOM – MORNING
scenes.summary: Marsellus sits at his dining table eating breakfast while talking on the phone, asking how badly Bonnie will freak when she comes home.

scenes.heading: INT. JIMMIE'S BEDROOM – MORNING
scenes.summary: Jules paces in Jimmie's bedroom on the phone, explaining the explosive Bonnie situation to Marsellus and demanding assurance that he'll handle the problem so they can chill Jimmie out and wait for help.

scenes.heading: INT. HOTEL SUITE – MORNING
scenes.summary: Winston Wolf, in a tuxedo at a hotel suite crap game, takes notes on the phone about the situation, asking when Bonnie is due and jotting down the principals' names.

scenes.heading: EXT. JIMMIE'S STREET – MORNING
scenes.summary: A silver Porsche races at 135 mph to Jimmie's house, stops on a dime, and a ringed finger touches the doorbell.

scenes.heading: INT. JIMMIE'S HOUSE – MORNING
scenes.summary: Jimmie opens the door to Winston Wolf, who introduces himself as a problem solver; Wolf enters, identifies Jules and Vincent, and confirms the clock is ticking with Bonnie due home in less than two hours.

scenes.heading: INT. JIMMIE'S GARAGE – MORNING
scenes.summary: The Wolf examines the car in silence, sends Jimmie to make coffee with lots of cream and sugar, then asks Jules about the car's mechanical condition.

scenes.heading: INT. KITCHEN – MORNING
scenes.summary: The Wolf drinks coffee and lays out the plan: Jules and Vincent will put the body in the trunk and clean the car's interior with cleaning products from under Jimmie's sink, working fast and thoroughly.

scenes.heading: INT. JIMMIE'S BEDROOM – MORNING
scenes.summary: Jimmie gathers linens while The Wolf talks on the phone about the 1974 white Chevy Nova, confirms twenty-minute arrival and that nobody will be missed, then reassures Jimmie that he wants to help and they'll get him out of this.

scenes.heading: INT. GARAGE – MORNING
scenes.summary: Jules and Vincent clean inside the car, both covered in twice as much blood as before; Jules angrily says he'll never forgive Vincent while picking up skull pieces and brain matter from the backseat.

scenes.heading: INT. CHEVY NOVA – MORNING
scenes.summary: The Wolf examines the cleaned car interior now lined with bedspreads and quilts, compliments Jules and Vincent on their work, and declares phase one complete before moving to phase two.

scenes.heading: EXT. JIMMIE'S BACKYARD – MORNING
scenes.summary: The Wolf orders Jules and Vincent to strip completely naked in Jimmie's backyard, then hoses them down while they stand in the cold morning air; he instructs them to toss their bloody clothes in Jimmie's trash bag.

scenes.heading: INT. JIMMIE'S GARAGE – MORNING
scenes.summary: The garbage bag is tossed in the trunk on top of Marvin and The Wolf slams it closed, then explains they're driving to Monster Joe's Truck and Tow with Jules riding with him in the tainted car and Vincent following in the Porsche.

scenes.heading: EXT. MONSTER JOE'S TRUCK AND TOW – MORNING
scenes.summary: Winston confirms everything is handled at Monster Joe's, introduces Jules and Vincent to Raquel who jokes about their volleyball outfits, then declines to drive them home since they live too far and tells them to take a cab.

scenes.heading: INT. COFFEE SHOP – MORNING
scenes.summary: Jules and Vincent sit in a coffee shop booth where Vincent eats pancakes while Jules nurses coffee and a muffin; they debate why Jules doesn't eat pork, discussing the filthiness of pigs versus the personality of dogs, before Jules explains his interpretation of the miracle and his decision to quit the life.

scenes.heading: INT. COFFEE SHOP BATHROOM – MORNING
scenes.summary: Vincent sits on the toilet reading his Modesty Blaise book, oblivious to pandemonium outside.

scenes.heading: INT. COFFEE SHOP – MORNING
scenes.summary: Pumpkin collects wallets in a trash bag during a robbery; when he reaches Jules and demands the briefcase, Jules refuses, Pumpkin aims his gun at Jules' face, but Jules reveals his own gun and takes control, disarms both robbers, and delivers a speech about his spiritual transformation before letting them leave with the cash but keeping the briefcase.
scenes.turningPointNote: Jules demonstrates his transformation by choosing mercy over violence, completing his character arc and the film's thematic resolution
